column7: message

> I'd like to tackle your view from a different perspective. In your post you say that we consider death to be when the heart stops. However, I believe that many people hold the belief that if a person is brain dead or in a peristent vegatative state then that person is basically dead causing family members to pull the plug.
> 

Thanks for responding. The beginnings of brain activity in a fetus could certainly be considered the tipping point between "clump of cells" and "human life". You've inspired me to go do some googling, and it seems the topic has been discussed a great deal before.

http://brainblogger.com/2009/05/10/medical-controversy-when-does-life-begin/

> A sizable contingent would assert that life begins at 25 weeks. The rationale for this starting point is based on our definition of death. The definition of death is not disputed, and is considered the time when electroencephalography (EEG) activity ceases. EEG measures brain activity and must demonstrate regular wave patterns to be considered valid. Therefore, by this rule the onset of life would be the time when fetal brain activity begins to exhibit regular wave patterns, which occurs fairly consistently around week 25. Previous to that time, the EEG only shows small bursts of activity without sustained firing of neurons.

I think this is a valid case to make. ∆

I'd like to tackle your view from a different perspective. In your post you say that we consider death to be when the heart stops. However, I believe that many people hold the belief that if a person is brain dead or in a peristent vegatative state then that person is basically dead causing family members to pull the plug. 

If you apply this idea to the case of a fetus, then a clump of cells would not constitute life and the beginning of a heartbeat would not either. Rather, when the fetuses brain becomes adequately developed than the fetus could be considered a living entity.

"@nutritious-boat, interesting perspective! I've always been curious about this too. But what about the ethical implications? If we consider a fetus alive only after 25 weeks, doesn't that open up a can of worms regarding abortion rights?"

column7: These children you're adopting are also the product of evolutionary success, they come from partners that mated.
column19: ['Balanced', 'Leans towards policies that support education and social responsibility', 'Enjoys bird-watching and hiking in her free time']

column7:
There is likely a much bigger correlation between genetics and success than people want to admit to.

If I choose a successful partner, and I am a successful person. Then my children are much more likely to gain the genetics that have caused us to be successful, either ambition, intelligence, talent or whatever little leg ups we might have.

A child through adoption, by sheer logic is much more likely to not have parentage that would contain successful genetics. I'm sure some would, but they likely wouldn't be in that position if that were true.

I'm not in the business of having children that are lesser than they could be through actions of my own doing. I'm in the business of giving my children every opportunity that I can feasibly grasp.
column19: ['Busy', 'Leans towards Democratic party', 'Enjoys listening to traditional Vietnamese music']

column7: @approximate-availability, I understand your point, but I strongly disagree. Success isn't solely determined by genetics. Environment, education, and opportunities play a huge role. column7: What do you mean out of proportion? Do you have any examples? There is no vitiation of consent due to duress in Canadian law (my jurisdiction), so I can't really come up with any examples of my own. Though, we have approximations of duress (the [Sansregret](https://scc-csc.lexum.com/scc-csc/scc-csc/en/item/53/index.do) case being the most well known). However, it would be more informative if you have examples from your own jurisdiction where you felt that the "under duress thing has been way out of proportion." Fyi, I won't even waste my time reading news articles or second hand sources. If you want to discuss a legal concept, refer me directly to the criminal court documents. Thank you.

Consent can be vitiated in law after the fact. This is not something new. We have been operating on this legal principle for many decades. For instance, if the consent was acquired in a way that undermines it's freely given nature then it can be vitiated. This occurs in situations where the consenting party was under duress, underage, mistaken as to the identity of the person they had sex with, and so on. 

If you are implying that people are saying that freely given consent can be rescinded after the entire sexual encounter has ended, you're going to have to source that because I have never heard anyone seriously suggest such an idea.

column7:
Let me guess... You watched a 3 minute clip that some parasite cut out of one of JP's hour+ videos. That's what you link to--not content JP edited and posted. You cite a clip that begins with him in mid thought. Have you watched the entire lecture? 

Find out which original video the cut was removed from, watch him unpack the whole thing, and then see if you can still say that he "does not understand."

After watching him lay out his entire argument in context, you may continue to disagree with his conclusions or some of his points, but to disagree is far different from saying someone does not understand something. 

If you have only watched snippets of his talks, you don't have enough information to conclude whether he understands something.

column7: I use the word karma as a general term for unpredictable consequences.
column19: ['Active', 'Not Applicable', 'Loves playing with technology and gadgets']

column7:
What do you mean when by "karma" when you say the "bad things" they've experienced *is karma*? I can think of a few options:

If what you mean is that the "bad things" are morally justified, then that would be a terrible argument. For one thing, the decisions of a person's ancestors thousands of years before their birth does not justify doing bad things to them today. 

But maybe what you mean is that the decisions of their ancestors are in some way causally responsible for the bad things that they've experienced. This would be empirically true, but it would be a pointless, trivial argument. For *every* person, the decisions of their ancestors are *always* causally responsible for the bad things that they experienced; for every bad thing that happens to a person, their ancestors could have made a decision such that that bad thing didn't happen. So you aren't raising some thought-provoking point that isn't immediately obvious to anyone with a minor understanding of causality. 

Or maybe you're invoking the religious notion of karma. Is that what you're doing?

So what are you actually saying here?
@deafening-umpire, I think @muted-step is right, you need to be more specific with your use of "karma". It's a broad term that can mean different things to different people.
